<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[es:Iwatsutsunoo]][[ja:イワツツノオ]][[zh:石筒之男神]]
{{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|qid=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}|offspring={{ill|Futsunushi|qid=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}}}



'''イワツツノオ'''（イハツツノヲ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である。

[[Image:Descendants of Izanagi and Izanami.svg|thumb|right|250px|Myth of the Birth of the Gods (The deities born by Izanagi and Izanami) [[Media:Descendants of Izanagi and Izanami.svg|View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]]

'''Iwatsutsunoo''' (also read Iwatsutsunowo) is a [[kami (Shinto)|deity]] appearing in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' it is written as '''Ishitsutsu no O no Kami''', and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as '''Iwatsutsu no O no Kami'''.

In the “Birth of the Gods” section of the ''Kojiki'', when [[Izanagi]] used the [[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi|Totsuka Sword]] to cut off the head of the fire god [[Kagutsuchi]], who had caused the death of his wife [[Izanami]], the blood that dripped from the tip of the sword fell onto a rock and gave rise to this deity. Prior to this, [[Iwasaku and Nesaku|Iwasaku no Kami and Nesaku no Kami]] (also called Iwaretsu no Kami and Neretsu no Kami) had been born in a similar manner. In the corresponding sixth alternative account of the same passage in the ''Nihon Shoki'', it is likewise recorded, and here Iwatsutsu no O no Kami is described as the ancestor of [[Futsunushi]]. In the seventh alternative account of the same passage in the ''Nihon Shoki'', it is stated that Iwatsutsu no O no Kami and Iwatsutsu no Me no Kami were born as the children of Iwaretsu no Kami and Neretsu no Kami, and that the child of these two deities was Futsunushi.

In a fragment of the {{ill|Tensho|qid=太白星}} quoted in works such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]'', Iwatsutsu no O no Kami is identified as the spirit of Taihaku (Venus), and Iwatsutsu no Me no Kami as the spirit of Chensei (Mercury).<ref>{{cite book|Japanese book|editor=Keizai Zasshi-sha |title=Kokushi Taikei|volume=Vol. 7|publisher=Keizai Zasshi-sha |year=1898 |section=Shaku Nihongi, Volume 5, Jutsugi 1|page=583|doi=10.11501/991097 |url={{NDLDC|991097/305}} }} (National Diet Library Digital Collection)</ref><ref>{{cite book|Japanese book|editor=Keizai Zasshi-sha |title=Kokushi Taikei|volume=Vol. 7|publisher=Keizai Zasshi-sha |year=1898 |section=Shaku Nihongi, Volume 6, Jutsugi 2|page=589|doi=10.11501/991097 |url={{NDLDC|991097/308}} }} (National Diet Library Digital Collection)</ref> Iwatsutsu no O no Kami and Iwatsutsu no Me no Kami are enshrined at some of the more than 160 Hoshimiya Shrines in Tochigi Prefecture. In addition, Iwatsutsu no O no Kami is also enshrined at Hayahoshi Shrine in Sumiyoshi, Toyama City, Toyama Prefecture.

== See Also ==

* [[List of Japanese deities]]

== References ==

{{reflist}}
*

{{Japanese mythology}}
{{Shinto}}

== References ==

{{reflist}}


*
{{神道 横}}
{{shinto-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|イワツツノオ|version=101094147|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[ja:イワツツノオ]]
[[zh:石筒之男神]]
{{wikidata link|Q9010200}}
[[Category:Amatsukami]]
[[Category:Japanese deities]]
[[Category:War deities]]
[[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
[[Category:need translation]]
